questions.question:
The determinant of the matrix given below is

[ 0   1   0   2 ]
[-1   1   1   3 ]
[ 0   0   0   1 ]
[ 1  -2   0   1 ]

A. -1
B. 0
C. 1
D. 2

understand.plain: To find the determinant of a 4x4 matrix, expand along a row or column that has the most zeros to minimise work. Here, column 3 has three zeros (rows 1, 3, 4 all have 0 in column 3), so expanding along column 3 leaves only one non-zero cofactor. Cofactor expansion reduces the 4x4 determinant to a single 3x3 determinant, which is easy to evaluate.

keywords.explain: Express the determinant as a sum of elements times their cofactors (signed minors) along any chosen row or column.
keywords.example: Expanding a 3x3 matrix along its first row gives det = a11*M11 - a12*M12 + a13*M13.

keywords.explain: The minor M_ij is the determinant of the (n-1)x(n-1) submatrix obtained by deleting row i and column j.
keywords.example: Deleting row 1 and column 3 of a 4x4 matrix gives a 3x3 minor.

keywords.explain: Expanding along a column with many zeros drastically reduces computation since zero terms contribute nothing.
keywords.example: Column 3 above has only one non-zero entry (row 2, value 1), so expansion gives a single 3x3 determinant.

understand.visual_alt: A 4x4 matrix with column 3 containing three zeros and one entry of 1 in row 2, ideal for cofactor expansion along column 3.

given.aim: Compute the determinant of the 4x4 matrix A = [[0,1,0,2],[-1,1,1,3],[0,0,0,1],[1,-2,0,1]].

terms.connects: Column 3 has only one non-zero entry — perfect for expansion.

terms.meaning: The cofactor of element a_{23}=1 is (-1)^(2+3) * M_{23} = -M_{23}
terms.connects: This is the only contributing term when expanding along column 3.

terms.meaning: 3x3 determinant after removing row 2 and column 3 from A
terms.connects: Computing this 3x3 determinant gives the final answer.

given.plan: Expand the determinant along column 3 (only one non-zero entry at row 2). Compute the resulting 3x3 minor M_{23} using cofactor expansion along its first column.

steps.title: Expand along column 3
steps.formula_raw: det(A) = sum_i a_{i,j} * (-1)^(i+j) * M_{ij}  for fixed column j
steps.note: Three zero entries make this column ideal for expansion.

steps.title: Form submatrix for M_{23}
steps.formula_raw: M_{23} = det of A with row 2 and col 3 deleted

steps.title: Compute M_{23} by expanding along column 1

questions.formula_note: Cofactor expansion along a zero-heavy column is the key technique — it reduces a 4x4 determinant to a single 3x3 minor computation.

questions.question:
The eigen vector(s) of the matrix

[ 0  0  alpha ]
[ 0  0  0     ]
[ 0  0  0     ]

where alpha != 0, is (are)

A. (0, 0, alpha)
B. (alpha, 0, 0)
C. (0, 0, 1)
D. (0, alpha, 0)
questions.to_find: All eigenvectors of the given 3x3 matrix

understand.plain: An eigenvector v satisfies Av = lambda*v for some scalar lambda. For a zero-heavy matrix, multiply the matrix by each candidate vector and check if the result is a scalar multiple of that vector. The matrix here has only one non-zero entry at position (1,3), so Av has a non-zero first component only if v has a non-zero third component — which can cause a contradiction unless the result still aligns with the original vector.

keywords.explain: A non-zero vector v such that Av = lambda*v for some scalar eigenvalue lambda. The direction is preserved (possibly scaled/flipped) under the transformation A.
keywords.example: If A*(1,0,0)^T = 0*(1,0,0)^T, then (1,0,0) is an eigenvector with eigenvalue 0.

keywords.explain: The set of all vectors x with Ax = 0. These are eigenvectors corresponding to eigenvalue 0.
keywords.example: For a zero matrix, every non-zero vector is in the null space.

keywords.explain: Since this matrix has rank at most 1, and it is 3x3, its characteristic polynomial must have 0 as an eigenvalue with high multiplicity.
keywords.example: A rank-1 3x3 matrix has eigenvalue 0 with algebraic multiplicity at least 2.

understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 matrix with only one non-zero entry alpha at position (row 1, column 3), sending most vectors to the zero vector.

given.aim: Find which of the given vectors are eigenvectors of A = [[0,0,alpha],[0,0,0],[0,0,0]].

terms.meaning: 3x3 matrix with A[1][3]=alpha (1-indexed), all other entries 0
terms.example: A*(0,0,1)^T = (alpha,0,0)^T, which is not a multiple of (0,0,1)^T
terms.connects: Only vectors with zero third component avoid being 'rotated' by A.

terms.meaning: Since A has rank 1, it has eigenvalue 0 with multiplicity 2 (or more).
terms.example: Any v in null(A) satisfies Av = 0 = 0*v.
terms.connects: Vectors with third component = 0 satisfy Av = 0, hence are eigenvectors for lambda=0.

terms.meaning: Vectors (x,y,z)^T such that Ax = 0, meaning alpha*z = 0, i.e., z = 0 (since alpha != 0)
terms.example: Both (alpha,0,0) and (0,alpha,0) have z=0 so Av=0.
terms.connects: Options B=(alpha,0,0) and D=(0,alpha,0) lie in null(A).

given.plan: Apply A to each candidate vector. If Av = lambda*v for some scalar lambda, it is an eigenvector. Vectors with z-component 0 give Av=0, confirming eigenvalue 0. Vectors with non-zero z-component give Av with non-zero x-component, which cannot be a multiple of the original unless the vector itself has the same ratio.

steps.title: Apply A to each candidate
steps.apply:
A*(0,0,alpha)^T = (alpha*alpha,0,0) = (alpha^2,0,0). Is (alpha^2,0,0) = lambda*(0,0,alpha)? That requires alpha^2=0 and lambda*alpha=0, but alpha!=0, contradiction. NOT an eigenvector.
A*(alpha,0,0)^T = (0,0,0) = 0*(alpha,0,0). YES, eigenvector with lambda=0.
A*(0,0,1)^T = (alpha,0,0). Is (alpha,0,0) = lambda*(0,0,1)? Requires alpha=0, contradiction. NOT an eigenvector.
A*(0,alpha,0)^T = (0,0,0) = 0*(0,alpha,0). YES, eigenvector with lambda=0.
steps.note: Options B and D yield the zero vector when multiplied by A, confirming eigenvectors for eigenvalue 0.

steps.title: Confirm the null space condition
steps.formula_raw: null(A) = {(x,y,z): alpha*z = 0} = {(x,y,0): x,y arbitrary} since alpha != 0
steps.apply: B=(alpha,0,0): third component=0, in null space. D=(0,alpha,0): third component=0, in null space. A=(0,0,alpha): third component=alpha!=0, not in null space. C=(0,0,1): third component=1!=0, not in null space.
steps.note: The null space has dimension 2, spanned by e_1 and e_2.

questions.formula_note: The eigenvector definition Av = lambda*v is the central tool — for a nearly-zero matrix, the null space directly yields all eigenvectors for eigenvalue 0.

questions.question:
Obtain the eigen values of the matrix

    [ 1   2   34   49 ]
A = [ 0   2   43   94 ]
    [ 0   0   -2  104 ]
    [ 0   0    0   -1 ]
questions.to_find: All eigenvalues of the given 4x4 upper triangular matrix

understand.plain: For an upper triangular (or lower triangular) matrix, the eigenvalues are simply the diagonal entries. This is because the characteristic polynomial det(A - lambda*I) factors into a product of (d_i - lambda) terms when the matrix is triangular, where d_i are the diagonal elements. This is one of the most useful shortcuts in linear algebra.

keywords.explain: A matrix where all entries below the main diagonal are zero. The diagonal entries are the eigenvalues.
det(A - lambda*I) = 0. For a triangular matrix this is (d_1-lambda)(d_2-lambda)...(d_n-lambda)=0.

understand.visual_alt: A 4x4 upper triangular matrix with diagonal entries 1, 2, -2, -1 and non-zero entries only above the diagonal.

given.aim: Find all eigenvalues of the 4x4 upper triangular matrix A with diagonal entries 1, 2, -2, -1.

terms.meaning: The elements a_{11}=1, a_{22}=2, a_{33}=-2, a_{44}=-1 on the main diagonal.
terms.example: These are directly read off: row 1 col 1 = 1, row 2 col 2 = 2, etc.
terms.connects: For a triangular matrix, these are the eigenvalues.

terms.meaning: All entries below the diagonal are 0: a_{ij}=0 for i>j.
terms.example: Row 2 starts with 0, row 3 starts with 0,0, row 4 starts with 0,0,0.
terms.connects: This guarantees the characteristic polynomial factors as product of (d_i - lambda).

terms.example: Each diagonal entry gives one linear factor, hence one eigenvalue.
terms.connects: The four roots are exactly the four diagonal entries.

given.plan: Recognise A is upper triangular. Apply the theorem: eigenvalues of a triangular matrix equal its diagonal entries. Read off lambda_1=1, lambda_2=2, lambda_3=-2, lambda_4=-1.

steps.title: Identify the triangular structure
steps.formula_raw: For upper/lower triangular matrix T, eigenvalues = diagonal entries {T_{11}, T_{22}, ..., T_{nn}}
steps.apply: Matrix A has zeros below the diagonal (a_{21}=0, a_{31}=0, a_{32}=0, a_{41}=a_{42}=a_{43}=0). Therefore A is upper triangular.
steps.note: The theorem applies directly — no determinant expansion needed.

steps.title: Read off eigenvalues from diagonal
steps.note: The characteristic polynomial is (1-lambda)(2-lambda)(-2-lambda)(-1-lambda)=0 with roots 1,2,-2,-1.

questions.formula_note: The triangular matrix eigenvalue theorem is the key shortcut — diagonal entries directly give eigenvalues, bypassing full characteristic polynomial expansion.

questions.question:
What are the eigenvalues of the following 2 x 2 matrix?

( 2   -1 )
(-4    5 )

A. -1 and 1
B. 1 and 6
C. 2 and 5
D. 4 and -1

understand.plain: For a 2x2 matrix, the characteristic equation is lambda^2 - (trace)*lambda + (det) = 0. The trace is the sum of diagonal elements and the determinant is ad - bc. Solving this quadratic gives the two eigenvalues. This is much faster than full cofactor expansion.

keywords.explain: Sum of diagonal entries of a matrix. For a 2x2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]], trace = a+d. Also equals the sum of eigenvalues.
lambda^2 - (trace)*lambda + det(A) = 0. The two roots are the eigenvalues.
keywords.example: For trace=7 and det=6: lambda^2 - 7*lambda + 6 = 0, giving lambda=1 or lambda=6.

understand.visual_alt: A 2x2 matrix with entries 2, -1, -4, 5 and its characteristic polynomial lambda^2 - 7*lambda + 6 = 0.

terms.meaning: trace(A) = 2 + 5 = 7. Equals the sum of eigenvalues.
terms.connects: This constrains the sum of the two eigenvalues.

terms.meaning: det(A) = (2)(5) - (-1)(-4) = 10 - 4 = 6. Equals the product of eigenvalues.
terms.connects: Together with trace, uniquely determines both eigenvalues.

given.plan: Compute trace = 7 and det = 6. Form the characteristic equation lambda^2 - 7*lambda + 6 = 0 and factor to find eigenvalues.

steps.title: Compute trace and determinant
steps.formula_raw: trace = a + d;  det = a*d - b*c
steps.note: Note: (-1)*(-4) = +4, not -4. Be careful with signs.

steps.title: Form and solve characteristic equation
steps.apply: lambda^2 - 7*lambda + 6 = 0.  Factor: (lambda - 1)(lambda - 6) = 0.  So lambda = 1 or lambda = 6.

questions.formula_note: The 2x2 characteristic polynomial lambda^2 - trace*lambda + det = 0 is the fastest route to eigenvalues, avoiding full determinant expansion of (A - lambda*I).

formulas.name: Cofactor Expansion of Determinant
formulas.plain: det(A) = sum_i a_{ij} * (-1)^(i+j) * M_{ij}  for any fixed column j (or row)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing an n x n determinant by expanding along a row or column with many zeros.

terms.means: element in row i, column j
minor: determinant of submatrix obtained by deleting row i and column j
sign of the cofactor at position (i,j)

formulas.trap: Always include the sign (-1)^(i+j). Forgetting this changes the sign of each cofactor.

formulas.name: Eigenvector and Eigenvalue Definition
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a given vector is an eigenvector, or defining eigenvalue problems.
formulas.trap: The zero vector is never an eigenvector by definition, even though A*0 = 0 = lambda*0 for any lambda.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Triangular Matrix
formulas.plain: Eigenvalues of an upper or lower triangular matrix = its diagonal entries
formulas.whenToUse: Any time you need eigenvalues of a triangular matrix — avoids full characteristic polynomial computation.

terms.means: diagonal entry in row i, column i
all entries below (upper) or above (lower) the diagonal are zero

formulas.trap: This only applies to triangular matrices. Off-diagonal entries do not affect eigenvalues but do affect eigenvectors.

formulas.name: Characteristic Polynomial of a 2x2 Matrix
formulas.plain: lambda^2 - trace(A)*lambda + det(A) = 0,  where trace = a+d, det = a*d - b*c for A=[[a,b],[c,d]]
formulas.whenToUse: Finding eigenvalues of any 2x2 matrix quickly using trace and determinant.

terms.means: sum of diagonal entries = a + d; also equals sum of eigenvalues
a*d - b*c; also equals product of eigenvalues

formulas.trap: When computing det = a*d - b*c, watch signs carefully when b or c is negative: (-1)*(-4) = +4, not -4.
